g.text: the network perimeter, not a statement of trust
Exactly two of the eight classes leave Agentry's trust boundary: 2 (provider egress) and 5 (callbackUrl).
Both are outbound from the gateway, both are always HTTPS, and both carry prompt or reply content. Class 3
crosses the boundary inbound. The other five never leave the cluster at all.
Class 5 is the one to reason about hardest.
Its payloads carry agent replies, which may contain PII, and its
receiver is chosen by the channel owner rather than by Agentry, so it terminates outside the trust boundary by
construction. That is why it is signed, allowlist-constrained, and re-resolved before dial with the checked IP
pinned into the connection: see the SSRF row of the threat model.
Class 2 supports custom CA bundles for enterprise environments. Class 5
is constrained by the deny-internal ranges or gateway.callbackUrl.allowlist, with pre-dial host re-resolution and
the checked IP pinned into the dial. Class 7 writes per-task completion ConfigMaps into
async response payloads plus per-replica budget partials into
log prompts or completions on any of these classes. This figure is about boundaries only:
for component responsibilities and the wiring between them, see System Architecture.
